participants.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official participant setting, inclusion criteria, non-anemic enrolled sample, and group/follow-up counts closely enough to be consistent.
participants.extracted: Infants living in Villa El Salvador, an urban settlement area in metropolitan Lima, Peru, were recruited through a household survey and clinic-based screening. Eligible participants were 4-6 months old at enrollment and had to be born at >2500 g with gestational age >37 weeks, be free of major malformations, genetic abnormalities, developmental problems, and known vision or hearing problems, and be expected to remain in the hospital catchment area for the next 12 months. Infants with hemoglobin concentrations ≤103 g/L were treated with iron and excluded, so the enrolled sample consisted of non-anemic infants. A total of 251 infants were randomized (122 to iron+copper and 129 to iron+copper+zinc); 108 and 101 infants, respectively, remained in the study at follow-up.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention details: daily 10 mg zinc syrup for 12 months alongside iron and copper, versus an iron-and-copper control without zinc.
interventions.extracted: Daily liquid micronutrient supplementation from 6 to 18 months of age. Infants were randomized to receive either iron plus copper (10 mg/day iron as ferrous sulfate and 0.5 mg/day copper as copper oxide) or the same iron plus copper supplement with added zinc (10 mg/day zinc as zinc sulfate). The supplements were given once daily in indistinguishable bottles, with a metered spoon delivering 2.5 mL per dose.

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes match the official secondary outcomes in substance and units, with the only differences being added hematocrit and urinary zinc plus more detailed time points.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure infant iron, zinc, and copper status, with biochemical assessments at 6 months (baseline), 12 months, and 18 months of age. Specifically, outcomes included hemoglobin concentration (g/dL) as an indicator of anemia/iron status, hematocrit as an anemia indicator, plasma ferritin as an indicator of iron stores, plasma copper concentration (umol/L) as an indicator of copper status, plasma zinc concentration (umol/L) as an indicator of zinc status, and urinary zinc concentration (umol/L) as an additional zinc-status measure. These were measured repeatedly at baseline (6 months), mid-intervention (12 months), and end of follow-up (18 months).
